title: Generic Character Sheaves for Parahoric Subgroups
speaker: Charlotte Chan
abstract: Lusztig's theory of character sheaves for connected reductive groups is one of the most important developments in representation theory in the last few decades. In this talk, we will describe a construction which extends this "depth zero" picture to give positive-depth character sheaves associated to generic data.

proof_stages.label: Deligne-Lusztig finite-field prototype
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the Deligne-Lusztig construction from a maximal torus and character to a virtual representation, whose generic irreducibility and regular-semisimple character formula motivate controlling the remaining character values by intermediate extension.

proof_stages.label: Parahoric representation analogue
proof_stages.summary: For an unramified torus in a p-adic group and a compatible building point, the speaker states the parahoric analogue: a smooth sufficiently generic character determines a unique irreducible representation with Weyl-orbit character formula on very regular elements, constructed through Moy-Prasad finite quotients and Deligne-Lusztig-type varieties.

proof_stages.label: Classical Lusztig character sheaves
proof_stages.summary: The Grothendieck-Springer correspondence is recalled as geometric parabolic induction from T to G, and Lusztig's theorem says that a generic multiplicative local system induces a simple perverse sheaf equal to the intermediate extension from the regular semisimple locus and decategorifies to the corresponding Deligne-Lusztig character.

proof_stages.label: Higher parahoric character-sheaf conjecture and obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Replacing the reductive group by the parahoric quotient GR gives an analogous correspondence and Lusztig conjecture for sufficiently generic multiplicative local systems, but the usual proof breaks because the projection is neither proper nor small.

proof_stages.label: Functorial generic-subcategory proof of simplicity
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem proving Lusztig's conjecture is obtained by studying parabolic induction as a functor on generic idempotent summands of equivariant constructible derived categories, where Fourier-transform arguments give equivalence, t-exactness, and preservation of simple perverse objects.

proof_stages.label: Decategorification comparison with XR cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The sheaf-function comparison is proved by a beefed-up Grothendieck-Springer description using sequences of Borels, giving equality up to a scalar and then determining that scalar by calculation on very regular elements.

proof_stages.label: Levi induction and iterative construction
proof_stages.summary: The torus-to-group theorem is extended to Levi-to-group parabolic induction, allowing an iterative tensor-and-induce procedure that builds many character sheaves for GR from Lusztig's depth-zero character sheaves and points toward applications to supercuspidal L-packets and stability.

examples.name: Negative sign in a Deligne-Lusztig curve case
examples.note: In response to a question about irreducibility of a virtual representation, the speaker notes that in a curve-type Deligne-Lusztig situation a nonsingular theta-isotypic part can lie in H^1, making the alternating-sum representation the negative of an irreducible representation.
examples.engagement_reason: she explains that in the Deligne-Lusztig curve case the relevant theta-isotypic cohomology lies in H1, so the alternating-sum convention produces a minus sign

passes.reason: The Deligne-Lusztig curve example is used to show that the virtual representation can be the negative of an irreducible representation rather than an actual irreducible one.
The Deligne–Lusztig curve with nonsingular theta is a standard nondegenerate case used to show the virtual induction can be the negative of an irreducible.
The Deligne–Lusztig curve case with nonsingular theta has the relevant cohomology in H^1, giving minus an irreducible and refuting the naive positive-representation reading.
The Deligne-Lusztig curve case is an ordinary rank-one-type case used to show the virtual representation can be minus an irreducible rather than positive.
It is used to show that Deligne--Lusztig induction may be minus an irreducible representation rather than an honest positive one.
the Deligne–Lusztig curve case shows the virtual representation may be minus an irreducible rather than an actual positive representation
Used to show a Deligne–Lusztig virtual representation can be the negative of an irreducible rather than an honest irreducible representation.
It is cited to show that a Deligne-Lusztig virtual representation can actually be the negative of an irreducible, while the curve case itself is not sporadic or extremal.

used to show that a Deligne–Lusztig virtual representation need only be minus an irreducible, not an honest positive irreducible.

examples.name: Dense character values for complex Lie groups
examples.note: The elementary fact that a continuous character of a finite-dimensional complex Lie-group representation is determined on a dense subset is used to motivate the replacement of analytic limits by algebraic-geometric intermediate extension over finite fields.
examples.engagement_reason: she reasons through the complex Lie group analogy by using continuity of the character and limits from a dense subset to recover all values

passes.reason: Dense character values for a finite-dimensional complex Lie-group representation are a standard continuity illustration, not a structurally exceptional object or a disproof case.
A finite-dimensional complex Lie-group representation is a standard setting where continuity lets dense character values determine all values.
Dense character values for an ordinary finite-dimensional complex Lie-group representation are used as an analogy via continuity, not as a structurally special or adversarial object.
Dense character values for a complex Lie-group representation are used as a continuity analogy, not as a structurally special or adversarial object.
A finite-dimensional complex Lie group representation with continuous character is used as a standard analogy for recovering character values from dense data.

used as an ordinary analytic analogy where continuity makes dense character values determine the character
used as an analogy where continuity lets dense character values determine the whole character, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Hyperspecial GL2 parahoric quotient
examples.note: The quotient for a hyperspecial point in GL2 is described as GL2 of a truncated power-series ring, making concrete why the Moy-Prasad quotient is a finite-dimensional algebraic group.
examples.engagement_reason: she applies the parahoric quotient construction to the hyperspecial GL2 case and identifies the resulting finite quotient explicitly as a GL2 over a truncated ring

passes.reason: The hyperspecial GL2 parahoric quotient is a standard maximally regular split integral-model example used to illustrate the finite quotient GR.
The hyperspecial GL2 quotient comes from a maximal good-reduction parahoric and is used only as a concrete quotient calculation.

examples.name: Split GLN model for GR, TR, and BR
examples.note: For split GLN the speaker identifies GR with GLN over O modulo a power of the maximal ideal, TR with the diagonal subgroup, and BR with the upper triangular subgroup, also indicating how twisted Frobenius recovers nonsplit tori.
examples.engagement_reason: she spells out the split GLN model concretely, giving GR as GLN over O modulo a power, TR as diagonal units, and BR as the corresponding upper triangular subgroup

examples.name: Upper and lower triangular BR not conjugate
examples.note: The upper and lower triangular analogues of BR in a truncated group need not be conjugate, illustrating the complication in comparing multi-Borel Grothendieck-Springer descriptions.
examples.engagement_reason: she only cites the upper and lower triangular BR subgroups as an obvious non-conjugate pair without proving or analyzing the non-conjugacy

passes.reason: The upper and lower triangular BRs are invoked specifically to show that the classical conjugacy of Borels fails for the fattened GR setting.
The opposite triangular BR subgroups are ordinary Borel-like subgroups deployed to show the usual conjugacy of Borels fails for GR.

examples.name: Regular supercuspidal L-packets
examples.note: In Q&A the speaker explains that regular supercuspidals built from depth-zero Deligne-Lusztig data fit Kaletha's L-packet parametrization and motivate using the new character sheaves for stability identities.
examples.engagement_reason: she describes regular supercuspidal L-packets and their compatibility with Kaletha’s parametrization as background/application, without computing or verifying a packet in a concrete case

passes.reason: Regular supercuspidal L-packets form a systematic generic class used as an application/compatibility target, not as an exception or obstruction.
